title: St. Agrippinus, St. Bishoy, St. Apollo of Bawit & St. Philemon the Musician
narrative:
title": "St. Agrippinus, St. Bishoy, St. Apollo of Bawit & St. Philemon the Musician

narrative": "title\": \"St. Agrippinus, St. Bishoy, St. Apollo of Bawit & St. Philemon the Musician

narrative\": \"St. Agrippinus of Alexandria, St. Bishoy the Great, St. Apollo of Bawit, and St. Philemon the Musician

On the fifth day of Yekatit, the holy Church places before the faithful four men whose lives demonstrate that the episcopate, the coenobitic life, the eremitic life, and the martyrdom of the ordinary layperson are not competing vocations but four expressions of the one consecration. Saint Agrippinus, the tenth Pope of Alexandria in the succession of Saint Mark, governed the apostolic see with the authority of one who had been formed in the Alexandrian tradition of learning and asceticism. His name means something like the one born feet-first, a detail the tradition preserves not as a curiosity but as a symbol of the reversed order of divine wisdom, in which the last shall be first and the one who seems disadvantaged at the outset is given the highest office. As patriarch, Agrippinus defended the deposit of the faith and cared for the poor with the generosity that the Alexandrian tradition demanded of its bishops.

Abba Bishoy the Great is one of the most beloved saints of the Egyptian and Ethiopian traditions: a hermit of Scetis who is said to have been chosen by Christ Himself for a singular intimacy — the tradition recounts that the Lord appeared to him and permitted him to rest his head upon His bosom, as the Beloved Disciple had done at the Last Supper. Bishoy lived in the desert for many decades, enduring heat, fasting, vigil, and the assaults of demonic temptation, and the monastery that bears his name at Wadi el-Natrun remains to this day one of the great living centres of Coptic monasticism. The Ethiopian Church venerates him as a father of the interior life, a man whose long discipline of the body produced a freedom and gentleness of spirit that drew disciples across the centuries.

Abba Apollo of Bawit is less celebrated but no less remarkable: the founder of the great monastery of Bawit in Middle Egypt, which in its heyday housed hundreds of monks and whose painted chambers, discovered by archaeologists in the modern era, revealed a visual theology of extraordinary richness. Apollo came to the desert as a young man after hearing a word from God and spent years alone before gathering the community that would become Bawit. The tradition records that he had a particular charism of joy — that visitors to his cell always left rejoicing and strengthened, as though his presence communicated something beyond what his words expressed. He taught that a monk who went about with a gloomy face was sinning against charity, for the Kingdom of God is joy in the Holy Spirit.

Saint Philemon the Musician is the discovery of this day's assembly — a mime and musical entertainer of the Roman world who was commissioned by a friend to present himself at a magistrate's office and offer sacrifice in his friend's place, while wearing a mask. Philemon put on the costume and the mask, but when he came before the presiding official and the order was given to sacrifice, he found that he could not do it: the grace of God had touched him in the masquerade, and he tore off his mask and declared himself a Christian, though he had not been one when he arrived. He was examined, confessed, and executed, his music exchanged for the greater music of martyrdom. The Ethiopian tradition loves such stories because they confirm what the fathers teach — that grace is not constrained by human readiness, and that the moment of encounter with the living God can occur anywhere, even in a theatre, even behind a mask, even in the middle of a deception. Amen.
